reasoning: The 2-year Treasury yield saw a sharp decline in late February 2025, dropping to 3.99% on Feb 28 due to weaker economic data. For the short-term (1 week / March 9), rapid mean reversion is favored following such a steep intraday/intraveek dip; the FRED linear model estimated a ~70% chance of an increase above the latest threshold. Therefore, the chance of the yield being strictly higher than its March 2 reference level is strongly positive for early March. As the forecast horizon lengthens into late spring and summer (April to August), uncertainty increases and the probability of being above 3.99% wanes because the overarching macro theme—an active Fed easing cycle—imposes downward pressure on short-end yields. By mid-2025 and into 2026, the potential for lower Fed funds rates perfectly counterbalances the chance of a re-acceleration or bounce, pushing the probability of an increase toward a neutral 0.50, satisfying the maximum uncertainty guidelines for long-horizon forecasting.
